segments.s081-g121: "دو منزل برفتند و گشتند باز" is a compact past narrative: "دو منزل برفتند" places the action over two stages (منزل = stage of a journey) with برفتند the 3rd-person plural past of رفتن ('they went/they departed'). The conjunction و links the two past verbs; گشتند باز is a past compound meaning "they turned/returned (back)" where گشتند (3pl past of گشتن, "to turn/return") precedes the adverb باز "back/again." Word order is verb-final for each clause (object/measure before verb) and the two verbs share the same subject (they).
segments.s081-g122: "کشید آن سپهبد براه دراز" follows in the same narrative: کشید is a past verb from کشیدن with a sense here of "to take/march/lead" (classical usage: to draw/take a course); آن سپهبد is the topicalized subject (that spahbed) placed after the verb for poetic word order; براه دراز is a prepositional phrase (بِ + راه "on the road") with دراز modifying راه as "the long road." The phrase thus means the spahbed set out / marched on the long road. Note: کشید in the given senses list does not show the motion sense explicitly, so this is an instance where the lexeme's contextual meaning is "to go/lead.".
segments.s081-g123: "وزان روی زال سپهبد به راه" opens with the adverbial phrase وزان (وز + آن) meaning "and from that" or "thereupon," signaling a temporal/result clause. روی here functions as a connective/adverbial sense roughly "on that account/thereupon" or "from that turn" (classical/poetic use), followed by the name and title زال سپهبد (Zāl the spahbed). به راه is the prepositional phrase "onto/to the road" (به = toward/into + راه = road). The whole segment sets up that Zal the spahbed took to the road as a result of what came before.
segments.s081-g124: "سوی سیستان باز برد آن سپاه" completes the action: سوی سیستان = toward Sistan (سوی = toward + سیستان place name). باز برد is a verbal phrase meaning "led back / brought back" (باز = back/again; برد is here the past form of بردن "to carry/lead/bring" used transitively), and آن سپاه is the direct object/topic "that army" placed after the verb for emphasis. The sequence thus says he led that army back toward Sistan. Note: the provided sense list for برد does not include the transitive "to lead/bring" reading, so the appropriate sense is supplied by context.
segments.s081-g125: "شب و روز با رستم شیرمرد" places an adverbial time phrase (شب و روز = night and day) before the accompanying phrase با رستم شیرمرد = "with Rostam, the champion." With (با) introduces the companion in whose company the following actions take place; the name and epithet follow the preposition in normal noun phrase order. The temporal phrase signals continuous or repeated activity.
segments.s081-g126: "همی کرد شادی و هم باده خورد" is two coordinated predications describing habitual/continuous activity. همی is an archaic/poetic progressive particle meaning "kept on / was continually"; کرد شادی literally "made joy" = "celebrated/made merry" where کرد is the past of کردن used causatively/performatively. و هم باده خورد = "and also drank wine" (هم = also, باده = wine, خورد = past of خوردن 'to eat/drink' used for drinking). The verbs are simple past forms used in a narrative but combined with همی to indicate ongoing/repeated action.
segments.s081-g49: وزان پس is a temporal/transition phrase ('thereupon/after that'); فرود آمد is a compound past verb meaning 'alighted/came down' (فرود + آمد). از introduces the source 'from', followed by پیل 'elephant' modified by the postposed adjective مست 'drunk/intoxicated' (noun + adjective order).
segments.s081-g50: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander'; بگرفت is past simple (3rd person) 'seized/took hold'; دستش is the object 'his hand' (دست + enclitic ـش = 'his'); بدست here is an adverbial/phrase 'in (his) hand' or literally 'by the hand', completing the sense 'took him by the hand'.
segments.s081-g51: همی is an adverbial particle adding immediacy/emphasis ('then/just'); بر سر و چشم او is a compound prepositional phrase 'upon his head and (upon) his eyes' (br + nouns + possessive او). داد is the past 'gave' and بوس is the noun 'a kiss' (داد بوس = 'gave a kiss' / 'bestowed kisses').
segments.s081-g52: فروماند is a past-state verb meaning 'remained/wore out' or 'were left exhausted' here applied to پیلان 'the elephants' (plural). The coordinate وَ links this clause to آوای کوس, a possessive/genitive phrase 'the sound/voice of the drum' (آوا + یـ + کوس).
segments.s081-g53: سوی کاخ is a prepositional phrase 'toward the palace'. ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'thereafter/after that'. نهادند is past 3rd-person plural 'they placed/set' used idiomatically with روی ('face') to mean 'they set/turned their faces' — i.e. 'they turned (their faces) toward'. روی here is the noun 'face' used figuratively as direct object.
segments.s081-g54: همه راه is an adverbial expression 'the whole way' or 'all the way'; شادان is a predicative adjective 'joyful' describing their state while traveling. و با گفت‌وگوی attaches another circumstantial element 'and with conversation' (با + گفت‌وگو = 'while talking / conversing').
segments.s081-g55: همه کاخها is 'all the palaces' (locative/collective context). تخت زرین is the noun phrase 'golden throne' (noun + adjective). نهاد is the past form of گذاشتن/نهادن 'placed/put' (here 'they placed' is implied), so the construction means 'they placed a golden throne in every palace.'
segments.s081-g56: نشستند is past 3rd-person plural 'they sat down' (classical past of نشستن); خوردند is past 3rd-person plural 'they ate'; بودند شاد is the past copula + adjective 'they were happy' (بودند = 'were', شاد = 'happy'). The three verbs are coordinated by و, describing successive or simultaneous actions.
segments.s081-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' placed at the clause start. آگاهی آمد is a simple past clause ('information/news came'); آمد is the past verb and آگاهی its subject. به سام دلیر is a prepositional phrase marking the recipient ('to Sam, the brave'), with the epithet دلیر following the name as is common in poetry.
segments.s081-g2: که introduces the reported subordinate clause 'that ...'. شد is the past of شدن meaning 'became'. پور دستان here functions as a name built from پور ('son/element in names') + دستان (a name); the following همانند شیر is a simile 'like a lion'.
segments.s081-g3: کس is the indefinite used here with a universal force ('no one'); اندر جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world'. کودک (literally 'child') here sets the timeframe ('from childhood'), and نارسید is a past-form negative ('did not reach/attain') forming part of the negative scope that extends into the next clause.
segments.s081-g4: بدین (poetic 'ب' + 'دین' = 'with this/so') is a demonstrative meaning 'such' or 'by this'. شیر modifies مردی to form the compound idea 'lion-like manhood' (شیرِ مردی). و connects گردی, another quality paired with مردی. ندید is the past negative 'did not see'; combined with the previous negative sense it yields the idea that there was no one who had failed to see such qualities (i.e. everyone had seen them).
segments.s081-g5: بجنبید is a past intransitive verb 'was stirred/moved' (poetic form). مرسام را دل is a poetic word order where را marks the following noun (دل) as the theme — effectively 'the heart of (or belonging to) Marsam' — and ز جای means 'from its place'. The whole phrase describes an emotional stirring.
segments.s081-g6: به دیدار is a prepositional phrase 'toward/for the meeting'. آن کودک is the object of that meeting ('that child'). آمدش is a compound of آمد + enclitic ش 'to him' meaning 'came to him'; رای here means 'counsel/plan/decision' and the construction آمدش رای means 'a plan/counsel came to him' (i.e. he formed a plan to go meet the child).
segments.s081-g7: سپه را is the definite object 'the army' (را marks definiteness). به سالار لشکر is a dative phrase 'to the commander of the army' (سالار governs the following noun لشکر in apposition, a common poetic order). سپرد is the past verb 'he entrusted/handed over'.
segments.s081-g8: برفت (poetic past 'he went away') denotes departure. The conjunction و links the next action. جهاندیدگان ('those who have seen the world') means seasoned or veteran men and is the object marked by را. ببرد here denotes 'take/carry (away)' — in narrative poetry this form expresses the subsequent action of taking those veterans with him.
segments.s081-g113: Literal order: object + verb. 'دو فرزند را' marks 'the two sons' as the definite object with the enclitic را. 'کرد پدرود' uses کردن + noun ('پدرود' = 'farewell') to mean 'bade farewell' (کرد is past tense). The conjunction 'و' links this action to the following speech, and 'گفت' is the past tense verb 'he said' introducing reported speech.
segments.s081-g114: 'که' is the complementizer 'that' introducing reported content. 'این پندها را' is the definite object ('these admonitions') with را. 'نباید' is the impersonal negative modal 'must not / should not', and 'نهفت' functions as the verb/adjectival notion 'be hidden' (here effectively passive/infinitival: 'ought not to be hidden'). The clause thus states an obligation not to conceal the counsels.
segments.s081-g115: 'برآمد ز درگاه' = 'arose/issued forth from the gate/threshold' (ز = 'from'). 'زخم درای' is a poetic nominal phrase: زخم (wound) + درای (possessing/‑having) = 'those bearing wounds' or 'the wounded'. The phrase names who/what came forth.
segments.s081-g116: This is a compact genitive/agentive description: 'ز پیلان' = 'from the elephants'; 'خروشیدن کرنای' = 'the roaring of the horn/trumpet' (خروشیدن = to roar, کرنای = trumpet-blast). Word order places the possessor/ source (ز پیلان) before the nominal phrase 'خروشیدنِ کرنای' — i.e. 'the trumpets of the elephants roared' or 'the trumpets' roar rose from the elephants.'
segments.s081-g117: Basic clause: subject 'سپهبد' then directional phrase 'سوی باختر' ('toward the west'), followed by the verbal phrase 'کرد روی' where کرد (past of کردن) combines with the noun 'روی' (face) to mean 'turned (his) face'. Classical Persian often places the direction before the verb.
segments.s081-g118: Two attributive nominal phrases joined by 'و': 'زبان گرم‌گوی' = 'a tongue that speaks warmly' (i.e., persuasive or flattering speech) where گرم‌گوی is a compound adjective modifying زبان; 'دل آزرم‌جوی' = 'a heart that seeks honor/glory' where آزرم‌جوی is adjective/agentive formed with -جوی 'seeker'. Together they describe his manner and disposition.
segments.s081-g119: Verb-first order: 'برفتند' (they departed/went) is placed at the start, followed by 'با او' ('with him') indicating accompaniment. 'دو فرزند او' is the subject 'his two sons' (او refers back to the father). Classical Persian permits this flexible word order (verb before subject).
segments.s081-g120: Two parallel adjectival phrases describing the sons: 'پر از آب رخ' literally 'full of water (of) face' — an idiom meaning 'faces full of tears'; and 'دل پر از پند او' = 'heart full of his advice' (او = 'his', i.e. the father's). 'پر از X Y' is the classical pattern 'Y full of X'.
segments.s081-g41: که‌ای here functions as a vocative particle/address ('O ...'); پهلوان جهان is the title 'champion of the world' with the noun before its modifier (typical poetic order). شاد باش is an imperative clause: شاد (adjective 'happy') followed by باش (imperative 'be'), giving 'be happy'.
segments.s081-g42: ز شاخ توام من is an archaic word order meaning 'I am from your branch/line': ز + noun ('from the branch') followed by a form expressing 'I am of you' (توام ... من). تو بنیاد باش is an imperative clause 'you be the foundation/support' (تو = 'you', بنیاد = 'foundation', باش = imperative 'be').
segments.s081-g43: یکی here = 'a certain' / 'one' used as an indefinite introducer. بنده‌ام is the speaker's humble self-reference 'I am a servant' (بنده + enclitic -ام). نامور سام را places the famous name in object/emphatic position with را marking the definite person to whom the speaker is servant.
segments.s081-g44: نشایم is the negative/privative form of شایستن ('to be worthy/deserve') in first person ('I am not worthy' / 'I do not deserve'). خور و خواب و آرام را lists the things of which the speaker claims no right — 'eating (خور), sleep (خواب), and rest/quiet (آرام)' — and the final را marks the whole series as the (definite) object of نشایم.
segments.s081-g45: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here giving continuing or resolute sense). پشت زین خواهم literally 'I will [place] the saddle on (my) back' (پشت = back, زین = saddle, خواهم used here as a future/auxiliary 'I will'). و درع و خود strings together 'and the cuirass/armor and myself' — خود emphasizes the agent ('I myself').
segments.s081-g46: همی again adds emphasis/continuity. تیر ناوک is a compound noun phrase 'an arrow with a (barbed) head' (تیر = arrow, ناوک = point/arrowhead). فرستم is the first-person present/future ('I will send/shoot'), and درود at the end is an exclamation 'hail' / 'greetings' placed as a closing formula.
segments.s081-g47: به چهر تو ماند همی چهره‌ام is topicalized: به چهر تو ('to/with your face') placed before the verb. ماند ('remains') is a stative verb and همی gives continuous/emphatic aspect; چهره‌ام ('my face' with the 1st-person suffix -ام) is the subject/predicate that remains like yours.
segments.s081-g48: چو can mean 'as/when/if' here introducing a conditional/similarity clause; آن تو باشد is a nominal clause with باشد ('be') as the verb. مگر is an excluding/contrasting particle ('except' / 'unless'); زهره‌ام is a nominal phrase with the 1st-person suffix (-ام) and functions as the contrasted predicate ('(if) I were Zohreh' or 'unless I am Zohreh'). The clause is elliptical/poetic — the copula may be implied.
segments.s081-g89: A coordinated noun phrase listing participants: 'من و رستم و اسب شبدیز و تیغ' = 'I and Rostam and Shabdiz the steed and the sword.' Word order is simple coordination with repeated 'و' between nouns; no verb appears in this segment, so it functions as the topic/subject cluster for what follows.
segments.s081-g90: This clause contains the verbal idea: 'سایه گسترد میغ' = 'the blade spread a shadow.' 'گسترد' is a past tense (classical Persian) verb 'spread'; 'میغ' at the end is the grammatical subject ('the blade') placed after the verb (poetic S–V–O/SOV flexibility). 'سایه' is the direct object 'shadow/shade'. 'برو' here is an adverbial/intensifying element meaning 'wide/over' modifying how the shadow is spread; 'نیارد' is an archaic/poetic element whose literal lexical senses in the provided list do not match the usage here — syntactically it behaves as a prefatory/intonational particle in this line (the main predication is carried by 'گسترد').
segments.s081-g91: 'کنم زنده آیین ضحاک را' — 'کنم' is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive from 'کردن' used here with a future/intent sense 'I will make/restore.' 'زنده' is an object complement meaning 'alive' (so 'make alive' = 'revive'), 'آیین ضحاک' is the definite object 'the rite/custom of Zahhak', and the enclitic 'را' marks that object as definite/specific.
segments.s081-g92: 'به پی مشک سارا کنم خاک را' — 'به پی' is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / following / on account of' (literally 'in the track of'); 'مشک سارا' = 'Sara's musk' (possessive noun phrase modifying motive); the second 'کنم' repeats the verb 'I will make' and takes 'خاک را' ('the earth/the dust' with object marker 'را') as its object. In sum: 'for Sara's musk I will (also) make the earth...' (poetic ellipsis leaves the final effect unsaid or implied).
segments.s081-g93: 'پر از خنده گشته لب زال و سام' — a poetic (inverted) clause: 'پر از خنده' = 'full of laughter' is a predicative phrase; 'گشته' is the perfect/participial form of 'شدن' used as 'became/has become'; 'لب زال و سام' ('the lips of Zal and Sam') is the grammatical subject/possessive phrase. Literal ordering is inverted for effect: 'The lips of Zal and Sam became full of laughter.'
segments.s081-g94: 'ز گفتار مهراب دل شادکام' — 'ز' (poetic variant of 'از') = 'from / because of'; 'گفتار مهراب' = 'the speech/words of Mehrab' (genitive/possessive); 'دل' = 'the heart' (subject); 'شادکام' = 'glad/fortunate' functioning as predicate/adjective. The sequence yields 'the heart became glad from Mehrab's words.'
segments.s081-g95: 'سر ماه نو هرمز مهرماه' — 'سر' here means 'head/start (of)' and with 'ماه نو' ('new month' = new moon) gives the temporal phrase 'at the beginning/new moon of the month.' 'هرمز' is the proper name of the actor, and 'مهرماه' specifies the month (Mehr). Word order is poetic; together they mean 'at the new moon of Mehr, Hormoz...'.
segments.s081-g96: 'بران تخت فرخنده بگزید راه' — 'بران' = 'on/thereon' (poetic 'بر آن'), 'تخت' = 'throne', 'فرخنده' = adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' modifying the throne or the action, 'بگزید' is the past tense of 'گزیدن' ('he chose'), and 'راه' = 'way/course.' The verb-final phrase thus reads 'thereon he chose an auspicious way/path,' i.e. 'he chose an auspicious course upon that throne.'
segments.s081-g25: This phrase lists two locative possessions: به + سر marks location (“on/at the head”), followed by what is placed there. برش here is an archaic/contracted form attached to the noun phrase indicating the possessor/context (effectively ‘on his…’); تاج is the noun ‘crown’. The second half uses و to link a second item: کمر (waist) with بر میان literally ‘upon the middle’ meaning ‘around the middle’ (i.e. a girdle/belt).
segments.s081-g26: Two more items are listed in parallel. سپر پیش uses پیش as a locative/adverb meaning ‘before/in front (of him)’. در دست is the idiom ‘in the hand’, where در is the preposition ‘in/inside’ and دست is ‘hand’. گرز گران is a noun + adjective sequence: ‘a heavy/weighty mace’. The clause is descriptive, no new finite verb here.
segments.s081-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. از دور is the prepositional phrase ‘from afar’. سام یل is a name plus epithet (‘Sam, the hero’). آمد پدید is the compound verb meaning ‘came into view/appeared’ (آمد, past of ‘to come’, + پدید ‘appear’). Word order is subject (name) before the verb phrase, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s081-g28: سپه (the host/army) is the subject. بر is the locative/prepositional particle ‘on/upon’ used here to introduce how the army was arranged. دو رویه رده is a noun phrase: دو (two) + رویه (here ‘faces/lines/ways’ in the sense of formation) + رده (ranks/rows) — i.e. ‘in two lines/ranks’. برکشید is a past tense verb ‘raised/lifted’ (or ‘put up/formed’), describing the army’s action. The verb is 3rd-person past.
segments.s081-g29: فرود آمد is a past, intransitive verbal phrase ‘came down/descended’ (آمد as auxiliary/verb + فرود as verbal/adverbial). از marks origin ‘from’. باره here names the platform/pavilion or occasion (classical usage), and مهراب و زال are coordinated names — the line means that from that platform Mehrab and Zal came down. Word order places the source phrase after the verb, a common narrative pattern.
segments.s081-g30: بزرگان is the plural nominative ‘the great ones/elders’. که introduces a relative clause ‘who…’, with بودند the past-tense copula ‘they were’. بسیار سال is an attributive phrase literally ‘many years’ — i.e. ‘of many years’, meaning they were of great age (advanced in years). The relative clause modifies بزرگان.
segments.s081-g31: یکایک is an adverb ‘one by one / each in turn’. نهادند is the simple past 3rd-person plural of نهادن (‘they placed/put’), here meaning ‘they bent/placed’ their heads. سر بر زمین is a frozen locative phrase ‘head on the ground’ (i.e. they prostrated/bowed). The implied subject is those just mentioned (the elders).
segments.s081-g32: This clause reports the acclamation. خواندند is the past 3rd-person plural ‘they shouted/called/sang’. سام یل is the addressee/name (‘Sam the hero’). آفرین is an interjection ‘bravo / well done / praise’ — here the elders cry out this praise to Sam. The initial word ابر is an honorific/intensifying element (archaic/poetic) attached in front of a name in this context and does not mean its ordinary ‘cloud’ sense.
segments.s081-g17: یکی لشکر از کوه تا کوه مرد is a nominal/telegraphic clause: یکی لشکر ('one/ a certain host') is the indefinite noun phrase, از ... تا ... is a prepositional span ('from mountain to mountain') and مرد (here a bare noun) functions as the predicate meaning 'men' (classical Persian often omits the copula 'were'). Word order is noun phrase + location span + predicate, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s081-g18: زمین قیرگون و هوا لاژورد is two parallel verbless descriptive phrases joined by و: 'the earth [was] pitch‑black and the air/sky [was] lapis‑lazuli.' Each adjective follows its noun (typical in Persian) and supplies the predicative property; the copula is elided for poetic brevity.
segments.s081-g19: خروشیدن is an infinitive/gerund used as a noun ('the roaring' or 'the cry'); it is followed by the agent/subjects تازی اسپان و پیل ('Arabian horses and elephants') in a noun phrase. There is no separate finite verb here because the gerund names the action that follows in the next clause.
segments.s081-g20: همی رفت is a classical progressive marker: همی (progressive particle) + past رفت gives 'kept going' / 'traveled on'. آواز is the thing that 'went' (the sound) and is placed after the verb by poetic inversion. تا چند میل is an extent phrase: تا = 'as far as', چند = 'several', میل here means distance (miles) — note the specific sense for میل (distance) is not listed in the provided senses.
segments.s081-g21: یکی introduces an indefinite ('one' / 'a certain') and precedes the noun phrase ژنده پیلی ('a ragged, sallow one'). ژنده is an adjectival/nominal descriptor 'ragged' and پیلی here is an adjectival color/quality 'sallow' (used to describe a person). The verb بیاراستند is third‑person plural past ('they adorned/they dressed'), so the whole segment reads 'they dressed/arrayed a certain ragged, sallow man.'
segments.s081-g22: This clause continues the action: ب + رو in برو is an archaic/poetic contraction (here effectively 'بر او' = 'upon him') and takes تخت زرین as its object 'the golden throne'; بپیراستند is another past plural verb 'they adorned/they fitted.' The literal structure is '[upon him] the golden throne they adorned' — i.e. they set/arrayed him on the golden throne. (Note: the printed برو in this segment functions as a contracted prepositional element rather than the verb 'go'.)
segments.s081-g23: نشست is the simple past 'he sat' / 'he took his seat' (verb first, as is common in poetry). از بر تخت زر is a classical/location phrase meaning 'upon the golden throne' (از بر is an archaic stacked prepositional form) and پور زال ('son of Zal') is the subject placed after the verb for poetic word order: 'Zal's son sat on the golden throne.'
segments.s081-g24: This is a descriptive prepositional cluster: (ا)با / با = 'with' introduces instrument/attribute phrases. بازوی شیر is a genitive construction 'the arm of a lion' (بازوی = 'arm'); و با کتف و یال repeats the preposition for parallelism 'and with shoulder and mane.' The repetition of با for each coordinate is a stylistic device to emphasize the lion‑like attributes.
segments.s081-g81: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for'/'that'); گیتی is the grammatical subject placed before the predicate; سپنجست is an archaic predicate/adjective (stative) describing the world. پر is an adjective meaning 'full (of)' and governs the following coordinated nouns آی و رو (an interjection/cry and 'face/appearance') joined by و. Word order is subject – predicate – complement, typical of epic Persian inversions and compact poetic phrasing.
segments.s081-g82: کهن شد is a simple past construction (شد 'became') with کهن as predicate 'became old.' یکی دیگر آرند نو follows: یکی ('one') is the actor/topic, دیگر آرند is an older/poetic verbal form (آرند — from آوردن) expressing 'bring' or 'they bring' (here narrative/poetic present or optative sense), and نو ('new') is the adjective placed after the verb phrase. The clause compresses 'one became old; another brings the new.'
segments.s081-g83: به here is a preposition marking instrument/means ('with/by'); می‌دست is an archaic nominal phrase meaning roughly 'with (the) wine in hand' or 'the wine-in-hand' acting as instrumental complement; بردند is past tense, 3rd person plural 'they took/carried.' The coordinate clause و مستان شدند has مستان as a predicative plural noun 'drunkards' and شدند (past 3pl of شدن) 'they became,' so the whole segment reads 'they took (it) in hand and became intoxicated.'
segments.s081-g84: ز is the poetic/genitive preposition 'from'/'because of'; رستم names the referent. سوی usually means 'toward' but here forms part of the locative/relational phrase 'سوی یاد دستان' = 'into the remembrance (or direction) of tales'; یاد دستان is a genitival compound 'the memory/recollection of (the) tales' (دستان = tales/stories). شدند is past 3pl 'they became,' so syntactically this says they 'became (turned into) [the] remembered tales of Rostam.'
segments.s081-g85: همی خورد is an iterative/imperfective past (همی + verb) meaning 'kept drinking' or 'was continually drinking'; مهراب is the subject or locus (here a noun in subject position), چندان is an adverbial 'so much/to that extent' modifying the amount, and نبید is the object 'wine.' The sequence is subject – iterative verb – adverbial – object: 'the mihrab/that one kept drinking so much wine.'
segments.s081-g86: که introduces the result/explanatory clause ('so that'/'that'); چون here = 'like/as'; خویشتن is the emphatic reflexive 'himself'; کس functions as 'anyone/any person' (under the scope of the negation that follows); به گیتی is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world'; and ندید is past simple negative 'he did not see.' Together: 'that he saw no one like himself in the world.'
segments.s081-g87: همی گفت is a habitual/past iterative 'he used to say' or 'he would say'; نندیشم is a negative subjunctive/contrary-to-fact first‑person form (ن- + نَندیشم) used in reported speech: 'I will not / ought not to think' or 'I do not think that I should think of...'; از here marks the object/topic 'concerning/of'; زال is the proper name and زر is the object 'gold.' The phrase is direct/reported speech compressed into the narrative.
segments.s081-g88: This clause continues the negation with a paired 'neither... nor' sense: the phrase is built with (implied) نه ... نه construction. از marks 'of/about' before سام (the name Sam); و is the coordinator 'and'; نز here functions as a condensed form corresponding to 'نه از' (not/of) leading into شاه ('the king'). با 'with' attaches the qualifying instruments 'تاج و فر' ('crown and glory') to شاه. Word order places the modifiers after the head (شاه با تاج و فر = 'the king with crown and glory').
segments.s081-g97: بسازید + سام + و + برون + شد + به + در is a short narrative clause. بسازید is the past/poetic form meaning 'built/made' (here: 'built'); سام is the subject/name after the verb (poetic word order). و simply links the two actions. برون شد به در is a compact motion phrase: برون (out) + شد (past auxiliary/verb 'became/went') + به در (to the gate/door). Together: 'Sam built (it) and went out to the gate.'
segments.s081-g98: یکی منزلی زال شد با پدر is a verbless / nominal clause where یکی منزلی 'one dwelling/place' is the thing established and زال شد uses شد in the sense 'became/took (up)' — common in older Persian to mark assuming a state. با پدر ('with his father') is an instrumental/companional phrase. The whole means 'Zal took up a dwelling with his father.'
segments.s081-g99: همی رفت is the customary epic progressive 'kept going/was going' (همی = continuously). بر پیل is the locative/onto phrase 'on (an) elephant' (بر + noun). دستم دژم is a compact descriptive phrase: دستم = 'my hand' (possessive enclitic -م) and دژم = 'grim/sullen' modifying it; such adjective placement is typical in poetry and gives a terse, emphatic characterization.
segments.s081-g100: به + پدرود کردن + نیا را + به هم expresses purpose/intent: به ... کردن (to do / to perform) with پدرود کردن 'to salute / to pay homage'. نیا را is the object of that action ('to the ancestor') and را marks it as definite/object. به هم ('together') modifies the action — 'to salute the ancestor together.' The initial به can be read as a preposition introducing the verbal noun (purpose) or as 'for/to'.
segments.s081-g101: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus said'. مر is the old/poetic object form 'to me' (preposed for emphasis), and زال را marks 'Zal' as the topic/agent in this inverted order (poetic word order: 'Thus Zal said to me'). کای is the vocative 'O' addressing the addressee, and پسر is 'son' — the direct address.
segments.s081-g102: نگر is the imperative 'look! / take heed'. تا introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause 'so that / in order that', and نباشی is the negative subjunctive/jussive 'you not be' (second person). جز از دادگر ('except/from a just one') completes the prohibition/limitation: 'be nothing except a just man.' The sequence expresses advice: 'Look, so that you be nothing but just.'
segments.s081-g103: به فرمان شاهان is an agent/means phrase 'by the command of the kings' — here به is best read as 'by' (agency). دل آراسته is a nominal phrase 'heart adorned' (دل modified by آراسته), an adjectival/participial attribute describing the person's inner state — 'his heart made/kept fair/adorned.' The two parts form the circumstantial background for the main verb in the next segment.
segments.s081-g104: خرد را گزین کرده is the core action: خرد را (wisdom, marked definite by را) + گزین کرده (perfect/periphrastic 'has chosen' — گزین + کردن). بر خواسته is a prepositional/advocative phrase meaning 'according to (his) desire' or 'in accord with the wish' (بر = 'according to / upon' + خواسته 'wish/desired'). Altogether: 'he chose wisdom according to his desire.'
segments.s081-g57: برآمد is a past tense verb meaning 'rose/appeared' (3rd-person plural or generic past in epic style). برین here is composed of بر + the adjectival/augmentative suffix -ین (ین) giving the sense 'lofty/supreme'; the following بر یکی ماهیان is a locative phrase 'upon/on one of the fishes' (بر = 'on/upon', یکی = 'one/a certain', ماهیان = 'fish' plural). Word order is poetic but literal: verb (برآمد) + modifier (برین) + locative (بر ... ماهیان).
segments.s081-g58: به رنجی نبستند هرگز میان is negative past: نبستند (ن- + بستند) = 'they did not bind/fasten'; به رنجی = 'with any hardship/trouble' (رنج + indefinite -ی gives 'some/any trouble'); هرگز = 'never' (adverb of negation) and میان = 'between' (postposition). Poetic inversion places the adverb and postposition after the verb; the object of 'بین' is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s081-g59: بخوردند is a past plural verb used here idiomatically of drinking ('they drank/partook'); باده is the direct object 'wine'. به آوای رود is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the sound of the stream' (به + آوای 'voice/sound' in the genitive relation with رود 'river'). In poetic Persian the verb can precede or follow its object; here the verb precedes the object.
segments.s081-g60: همی + past-verb (گفت) expresses iterative or habitual action ('would say' / 'kept saying'); هر یک = 'each one' is the subject, به نوبت = 'in turn' is an adverbial phrase, and سرود is the accusative noun 'song/ode' (object). Note in epic poetry گفت can stand for 'sang/recited' in context though its dictionary entry here is nominal — syntactically it is the past tense of گفتن 'to say/sing'.
segments.s081-g61: به یک گوشه تخت is a locative phrase 'at one corner of the throne' (به marking location). دستان here (poetically) refers to the bards/minstrels or their songs; نشست is the past verb 'sat' (نشست used as 'sat down'). Word order is locative + subject + verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s081-g62: دگر گوشه = 'the other corner'; رستمش is رستم with an enclitic -ش (an archaic/epic possessive/pronominal clitic often attached to names in Ferdowsi) and functions as the subject 'Rostam' here. گرزی is an indefinite noun 'a mace' and به دست = 'in hand' (به marking instrument or locus). The phrase means 'in the other corner Rostam (had) a mace in hand.'
segments.s081-g63: به پیش اندرون سام is a locative/agentive fronting: به پیش = 'before/in the presence of', اندرون = 'the inner (chamber)', سام = proper name. گیهان گشای is an imperative construction: گیهان 'world(s)' as the object followed by گشای, an imperative 'open!' (archaic/poetic form). The verb appears after its object, a typical poetic inversion.
segments.s081-g64: فرو هشته is 'let down/let fall (فرو) a lock/tuft (هشته)' — فرو here indicates downward motion. از تاج پر همای = 'from the crown full of the Simurgh's feathers' with از marking source, تاج 'crown' the noun, پر used adjectivally as 'full (of)', and همای 'the Simurgh (mythical bird)' modifying 'پر'. The sequence gives source + description after the imperative.
segments.s081-g65: ز here means 'at/regarding' (poetic use of از/به) and introduces the thing causing astonishment. همی + بماند is an archaic/habitual present construction: همی marks continuous or repeated action and بماند (from ماندن) is 3rd-person singular 'remains/is left' — the grammatical subject is omitted but understood (the creature being described).
segments.s081-g66: برو here functions as a 3rd-person present habitual 'it goes/it moves' (classical contraction of برود), describing a repeated reaction. هر زمان introduces a general temporal clause 'whenever', and بخواند is 3rd-person subjunctive/indicative used in such clauses meaning '(one) utters/calls' — so: 'whenever the name of God is called, it goes.'
segments.s081-g67: بدان is an archaic/compact prepositional phrase ('به + آن') meaning 'with/by that' and introduces an enumerated list of body-parts. The segment simply enumerates those parts (بازوی, یال, آن پشت, شاخ) joined by و.
segments.s081-g68: میان here = 'in the middle' or 'its middle/waist'. چون introduces a simile 'like', with قلم ('pen') as the point of comparison (i.e. slender). After the comma سینه 'chest' is given and بر فراخ ('broad/upon wide') describes it — a copula-less classical clause meaning 'its chest was broad.'
segments.s081-g69: دو رانش: رانش is an archaic/clitic form meaning 'his ران (thigh/haunch)' (the -ش is the 3rd-person possessive), so 'his two haunches'. چو introduces the comparison 'like', ران هیونان = 'the haunches of beasts', and ستبر is the predicate 'stout/solid'.
segments.s081-g70: دل شیر نر is a genitive phrase 'the heart of a male lion' (نر qualifying شیر). دارد is 3rd-person present 'has/possesses'. و links the clauses, and زور ببر = 'the strength of a tiger' (ببر as noun in genitive relation).
segments.s081-g71: بدین is the classical compound 'به + این' meaning 'by/with this' and introduces the qualities being listed (خوب رویی: 'beautiful/fair face'; این فر: 'this royal glory'; و یال: 'and mane'). The phrase enumerates attributes in a compact, poetic sequence.
segments.s081-g72: ندارد is the negative present 'does not have' (here meaning 'there is not/has no one'). کس 'anyone/person' with از پهلوانان 'of the champions' forms the group being quantified, and همال is used in the sense 'equal/peer' — so the clause means 'no one among the champions is his equal.'
segments.s081-g33: چو here introduces a temporal clause meaning “when/as.” گل چهره is a poetic compound ‘flower‑face’ = ‘face like a flower’ (noun + noun attributive), and سام یل is the name with the epithet ‘یل’ (hero) after it. بشکفید is an archaic/past 3rd‑person form “bloomed” (classical Persian -ید ending) with the subject implied (Sam), so the whole segment reads “when Sam the hero’s face bloomed like a flower.”
segments.s081-g34: The second چو again means “when.” The subject is continued from the previous clause (Sam). بر پیل is locative/locution “on the elephant,” بر بچه شیر -> “on (or upon) a lion‑cub”; دید is past 3rd‑person “he saw.” Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase(s) before the verb, and the verb (دید) completes the clause.
segments.s081-g35: چنان means “thus/so.” همش is a contracted/object form meaning “all of it.” بر پیل پیش آورید is a verbal phrase ‘brought before the elephant’ — آورید here is a classical past form “brought” (third person) and پیش marks placement ‘before/in front of.’ The phrase describes an action performed by the (unspecified) agents.
segments.s081-g36: نگه کرد is a compound verbal expression: نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (past “made/did”) = “he looked” or “he fixed his gaze.” The conjunction و links the next clause. با تاج و تختش is an instrumental/accompaniment phrase “with (his) crown and throne” (تختش = تخت + possessive -ش), and بدید is past “he saw.” The subject (he = Sam) is omitted but carried over from prior clauses.
segments.s081-g37: یکی is an indefinite subject “someone/a certain one.” آفرین here is a noun/acclamation “praise” (not the verb ‘to create’), and کرد is the past verb “did/made” — together آفرین کرد = “gave praise/praised.” سام دلیر is the object with an epithet after the name: “Sam the brave.”
segments.s081-g38: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause (“for/because”). تهما هژبرا is treated as a compound proper name (two name elements). بزی here appears as an epithet/descriptor attached to the name; شاد دیر is a predicative phrase: شاد “happy,” دیر (classical placement) “long [of time]” — i.e. “had been happy for a long time.” Word order is archaic/poetic but the grammar is subject–predicate within the subordinate clause.
segments.s081-g39: ببوسید is past 3rd‑person “he kissed.” رستمش is the name رستم with the enclitic -ش (here forming a genitive/associative link to the following noun), so رستمش تخت‌ای شگفت reads as “Rostam’s wondrous throne” (تخت‌ای = “a/that throne” with the attributive -ای, شگفت = “wondrous”). The verb precedes its object phrase in the poetic line but is read as standard past action.
segments.s081-g40: نیا را uses را as the object/indirect‑object marker (“to/for Niyā”). یکی نو ستایش is the noun phrase “a new praise” (یکی = one/a, نو = new, ستایش = praise) and گرفت is the past verb “took/received” — here idiomatically “offered/expressed praise.” Word order is object (to‑phrase) + (a) new praise + verb, typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s081-g105: همه‌ساله is an adverbial phrase 'each year/annually' placed at the start. The sequence بر بسته دست is a prepositional/circumstantial phrase: بر + past participle بسته + noun دست = 'with (a) hand bound' (poetic word order: preposition before participle and noun). از بدی is a simple prepositional phrase 'from/against evil' showing the reason or direction of the action.
segments.s081-g106: همه روز is another adverbial 'every day / day by day'. جسته is the past participle / perfect of جستن used here predicatively ('sought' or 'took to') and precedes its object in this poetic line. ره ایزدی is the object phrase 'the divine way/path' with the adjective ایزدی postpositive.
segments.s081-g107: چنان دان is an imperative/formulaic exhortation 'consider/know thus' (دان used in the sense 'regard/consider'). که introduces the content clause. بر کس نماند جهان is a negated predication with poetic word order: نماند (does not remain) + جهان (the world) + بر کس ('for/to anyone' — بر here functions as a dative/prepositional marker).
segments.s081-g108: یکی here functions as 'one thing' or 'first/one' introducing what follows. بایدت is the present/modal construction 'it is necessary for you' with the enclitic ت marking 'to you'. آشکار و نهان are two nominal complements (the manifest and the hidden) that are presented as what you must attend to or know.
segments.s081-g109: برین پند من is the phrase 'on this counsel of mine' (بر + این = 'regarding/with this'). باش is an imperative 'be/adhere' (i.e., 'hold to it'). و مگذر ازین is a coordinated negative command: مگذر is the negative imperative 'do not pass/let pass', and ازین ('from this') repeats the demonstrative 'this' as the object of the negation.
segments.s081-g110: بجز introduces an exception 'except'. بر ره راست is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the straight path' (ره راست = 'straight/right way'). مسپر is an imperative form of سپردن ('entrust' / 'commit') used here; زمین is the indirect object 'to the earth/on earth' (i.e. 'to any on earth' in context). Poetic word order places the imperative after the exception phrase.
segments.s081-g111: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/saying that'. در دل is a locative phrase 'in (the) heart'. ایدون is a verb meaning 'comes/appears' (an archaic/poetic form), and گمانم is the speaker's noun 'my suspicion/idea' (گمان with the possessive -م). همی here is a modal/affirmative particle adding immediacy or emphasis to the statement.
segments.s081-g112: که (again) introduces the subordinate content. آمد is the verb 'comes/arrives' (used here to express that something will happen). به تنگی زمانم is a locative/temporal phrase 'upon the narrowing of my time' (تنگی = 'narrowness/shortness', زمانم = 'my time'), i.e. 'when my time grows short'. همی at the end is a particle reinforcing the assertion or ongoing expectation.
segments.s081-g9: چو here is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. مهرش is مهر + enclitic ـش ('his'), i.e. 'his favour/affection'; سوی is the preposition 'toward' placed before the person. پور دستان is a personal name built from پور + دستان (name-element treated as a unit); word order places the direction phrase before the verb. کشید is the past tense; in epic Persian کشیدن can mean 'to draw/turn/lead (toward)' — it is the finite verb closing this clause and links by sense to the next clause where the same verb repeats its action on the army.
segments.s081-g10: سپه را سوی زاولستان کشید is a transitive clause with را marking سپه (the definite object 'the army'). سوی + place gives the goal/direction; the verb کشید (past of کشیدن) comes at the end as is normal in Persian and here means 'he led/moved (it) toward'.
segments.s081-g11: چو again = 'when'. زال is the proper name (here the hero Zāl). آگهی یافت is a light verb construction: آگهی 'news/information' + یافت (past of یافتن) = 'received news/learned'. بر بست کوس is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic phrasing: بر + بست + کوس — in epic usage بَستنِ کوس can mean 'to sound/beat the war-drum'; the phrase functions as the predicate of this clause.
segments.s081-g12: ز is the poetic form of 'from' introducing the cause ('from/because of the army'). زمین گشت is 'the earth became/was turned' (گشت = became/turned); چون + noun is the simile marker 'like/as', so چون آبنوس = 'black as ebony'. The clause orders the cause first (ز لشکر) then the subject+verb+simile, a normal inverted arrangement in poetry.
segments.s081-g13: خود و گرد مهراب کابل = 'he himself and the group (گرد) of Mehrāb of Kabul'. Here گرد functions as 'group/retinue/companions (around someone)' and follows the coordinating و. مهراب کابل is a name + place (Mehrāb of Kabul); punctuation and word order in epic Persian often place such modifiers in sequence before the verb phrase that follows.
segments.s081-g14: پذیره شدن is a deverbal noun/infinitive phrase 'to be received; reception/acceptance' (پذیره = reception + شدن = 'to become / to be (received)'). را is the object marker making 'پذیره شدن' the object of نهادند ('they set/placed'); نهادند رای is a fixed classical collocation meaning 'they put/set a counse l' or more loosely 'they resolved/decided'. In short, the whole segment expresses that they decided on accepting/receiving.
segments.s081-g15: بزد is the simple past 'he struck/struck off'. مهره در جام is a short action-clause: مهره (a bead/game-piece/token) + در جام 'into the cup' — an image of making a decisive move or signaling; و links to برخاست غو where برخاست (past) = 'rose/arose' and غو = 'uproar/clamour'. The verb order (verb-final) and compactness are typical of epic diction.
segments.s081-g16: برآمد ز هر دو = 'arose from both' (برآمد = 'issued/arose', ز = 'from', هر دو = 'both'). سپه دار و رو is a compressed coordinate: سپه‌دار literally 'army-holder/leader' (commander) + و رو ('and faces' / 'and appearance' — here 'رو' refers to people/men or their faces/visages); together they name the two groups from whom the uproar came. Note that compound titles like سپه‌دار are common in epic Persian and word-meanings are often context-determined.
segments.s081-g73: بدین شادمانی is a prepositional/instrumental phrase (بدین = 'with this' = به + این) placed before the verb for emphasis. کنون is an adverb 'now' modifying the following verb. می‌خوریم is the present (prefix می-) first‑person‑plural verb 'we drink' (literal 'we are drinking' or 'we drink').
segments.s081-g74: به introduces an instrument/means ('by/with'); می‌جان here is a compressed poetic compound and functions as a content phrase (literal possibilities: 'wine of life' or an endearment), followed by اندوه 'sorrow' marked as a definite object with را. بشکریم is a jussive/hortative first‑person‑plural form (subjunctive flavor: 'may we ...' / 'we shall ...') — in context it expresses acting upon or removing sorrow; the exact classical root is archaic/poetic.
segments.s081-g75: به زال آنگهی گفت is 'he said to Zāl then' (به + proper name = 'to Zāl'; آنگهی = 'then/thereupon'). گفت is the speech verb (classical past), and تا here introduces a quantity/limit ('تا صد نژاد' = 'as many as a hundred lineages' / 'ask up to a hundred families'). نژاد = 'lineage, race, family'.
segments.s081-g76: بپرسی is a jussive/subjunctive second‑person form ('you ask' / 'go and ask') used after گفت to report the command. کس here is used negatively in the sense 'no one' in the following clause. این را ندارد بیاد literally 'does not have this in memory' (ندارد = 'does not have'; بیاد = 'in memory/of recollection'), so the clause means 'no one remembers this.'
segments.s081-g77: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). کودک ز پهلو برون آورند is a subordinate clause: ز = poetic 'from', پهلو = 'the side/flank', برون = 'out', and آورند is third‑person‑plural present/subjunctive 'they bring' or 'they are to bring' (classical narrative uses آورند for a general/habitual/optative sense), so 'that they bring the child out from the side.'
segments.s081-g78: بدین نیکویی is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/with this goodness' (بدین = 'by this'); چاره 'remedy/means' is the noun, and چون here is the interrogative 'how'. آورند again is third‑person‑plural 'they bring/produce' but here means 'how can they effect/bring about such a remedy' (i.e. 'how could they contrive a solution').
segments.s081-g79: بسیمرغ is the name 'Simurgh' (mythical bird). بادا is an optative particle meaning 'may it be' or 'let there be' (classical wish/benediction). هزار آفرین is an idiomatic phrase 'a thousand praises' or 'a thousand blessings' directed at the Simurgh.
segments.s081-g80: که here introduces the reason/explanation ('for/that'); ایزد = 'God/Providence'. ورا is the enclitic object 'to him' (و + را variant = 'him/it'), ره = 'way/path', and نمود is the past tense 'showed' (نمودن → نمود = 'showed/manifested'). اندرین = 'in this' or 'among these (matters)'. Altogether: 'for God showed him the way in this.'

sentences.s081-s61: They traveled two stages and then turned back; that spahbed marched off along the long road.
sentences.s081-s62: And from that time Zal, the spahbed, took to the road and led that army back toward Sistan.
sentences.s081-s63: Night and day he made merry with Rostam the champion and likewise drank wine.
sentences.s081-s25: Thereupon he alighted from the drunken elephant, and the commander seized his hand.
sentences.s081-s26: He then pressed kisses upon his head and eyes; the elephants grew weary and the drum sounded.
sentences.s081-s27: Thereupon they turned toward the palace; all the way they were joyful and conversing.
sentences.s081-s28: In every palace they set a golden throne; they sat, ate, and were glad.
sentences.s081-s1: When news reached the brave Sam that Pour-Dastan had become like a lion.
sentences.s081-s2: There was no one in the world who, from childhood on, had not seen such lion-like manhood and prowess.
sentences.s081-s3: Marsam's heart stirred from its place; he moved toward meeting that child, and a counsel (a plan) came to him.
sentences.s081-s4: He entrusted the army to the commander of the troops, departed, and took the seasoned veterans with him.
sentences.s081-s57: He bade his two sons farewell and said that these counsels must not be hidden.
sentences.s081-s58: From the gate came forth the wounded, and from the elephants rose the trumpets' roar.
sentences.s081-s59: The spahbed turned his face westward, with a persuasive tongue and a heart seeking honor.
sentences.s081-s60: Two of his sons went with him; their faces full of tears, their hearts full of his counsel.
